ks1.objective: Pass the ball with your team and keep it away from the player in the middle.
successCriteria: Moves to a space to receive
Joins back in quickly if they become the 'piggy'

lks2.objective: Keep possession through movement, support and quick reactions when the ball is lost.
successCriteria: Constant scanning and movement off the ball
Supports the ball-carrier with angled runs
Reacts positively when becoming the defender

uks2.objective: Maintain possession under restricted touches and rotate roles seamlessly when the defender wins it back.
successCriteria: Plays within the touch limit consistently

space.description: All children play inside the same area. Groups of three or four — two/three attackers and one defender per group. Each group has one football.

value.name: Positivity
value.text: Staying positive when things feel tricky. Everyone will lose the ball at times or find themselves in the middle — what matters is how they react. Encourage them to keep smiling, keep trying and jump straight back into the game. Praise good effort, positive body language and a willingness to go again.

steps: Within each group, the attackers (2 or 3) work together to keep possession.
The defender applies pressure and tries to win it back.
All players are free to move around the area — constant scanning, movement and support.
When the defender wins possession, they immediately join the attacking team.
The player who lost the ball becomes the new defender.
The game continues without stopping — quick reactions and a positive mindset when possession is lost.
progressions: Begin with the attackers throwing and catching the ball.
Progress so attackers are allowed unlimited touches with the ball.
Challenge attackers to use 5 touches only, or they become the player in the middle.
Continue to set lower touch limits — down to 2 touches if appropriate.
questions: Where should you move to be a useful passing option?
What body shape helps you receive on the half-turn?
How can you make the defender's job harder before you receive the ball?

step.space: Reduce area size to increase pressure and speed of decisions
step.task: Challenge attackers to play one-touch passes when possible
step.equipment: Smaller marked zone within which top performers must stay (e.g. half-pitch)
step.people: Add a second defender to some groups for a 2v2 challenge

base.showmanship: Highlight and praise children who respond positively when losing the ball. Make it clear that mistakes are part of the game — what matters is the next action. Link back to positivity, and explain that positivity is most important in the face of adversity.
